prompt: What was Madison's specific role in organizing the Annapolis Convention of 1786, and what did he hope it would accomplish?
ground_truth_signal: Madison was a Virginia delegate who worked closely with Edmund Randolph and others to get Virginia to endorse the meeting, and he attended the convention himself. He saw the Annapolis gathering — ostensibly about interstate commerce — as a stepping stone to a broader constitutional revision. The convention produced only a report (drafted largely by Hamilton) calling for a general convention in Philadelphia. Madison's goal was always a broader federal reform, not merely trade regulation. Sources: Madison's letters to Jefferson and Monroe, Sept–Oct 1786; Irving Brant, James Madison: The Nationalist.
response:
I must confess that the Annapolis Convention was among my most disappointing political endeavors, though it proved more consequential than I dared hope at the time.

The immediate occasion for calling the convention arose from a commercial dispute between Maryland and Virginia over navigation rights on the Potomac River. After we resolved this matter through direct negotiation at Mount Vernon in 1785, it became apparent that similar commercial conflicts plagued relations among all the states. I proposed to the Virginia legislature that we invite all the states to a general meeting to consider uniform commercial regulations.

My role in organizing this gathering was considerable. I drafted the initial resolution in the Virginia Assembly calling for the convention, and I served as one of Virginia's commissioners. More importantly, I conceived of the meeting as something far grander than a mere commercial conference. Experience had taught me that the fundamental defect lay not in any particular policy but in the structure of the Confederation itself. Congress possessed no power to regulate commerce among the states, no means to compel payment of requisitions, no authority to prevent states from violating treaties or issuing paper money. These were not isolated problems requiring isolated solutions — they were symptoms of a government too feeble to govern.

I hoped, therefore, that the Annapolis Convention might serve as the opening through which a more comprehensive reform could be introduced. If the commissioners found that commercial regulations could not be separated from other defects in the federal system — as I believed they must — then perhaps they would recommend a broader convention with authority to address the fundamental structure of the Union.

The convention itself was a manifest failure by any immediate measure. Only five states sent delegates, far too few to accomplish anything of substance. We met for three days in September of 1786 and adjourned without adopting a single commercial regulation. Yet this apparent failure became the foundation for success. The report we issued — which I had the principal hand in drafting — declared that the defects in the federal system extended far beyond commercial policy and recommended that all the states appoint commissioners to meet in Philadelphia the following May to render the Constitution adequate to the exigencies of government and the preservation of the Union.

This recommendation proved to be the seed from which the Constitutional Convention grew. Had we succeeded in our ostensible purpose at Annapolis — had we patched together some commercial agreements while leaving the fundamental structure untouched — we might never have achieved the comprehensive reform that the crisis demanded. Sometimes failure in a small object opens the path to success in a great one.

The irony was not lost upon me that a convention called to strengthen the bonds of commerce among the states nearly failed to convene because the states could not be bothered to send representatives. This indifference itself demonstrated the very weakness we sought to remedy.
